items.document:
Challenges and Opportunities in NLP Benchmarking

Over the last years, models in NLP have become much more powerful, driven by advances in transfer learning . A consequence of this drastic increase in performance is that existing benchmarks have been left behind. Recent models "have outpaced the benchmarks to test for them" ( AI Index Report 2021 ), quickly reaching super-human performance on standard benchmarks such as SuperGLUE and SQuAD . Does this mean that we have solved natural language processing? Far from it. However, the traditional practices for evaluating performance of NLP models, using a single metric such as accuracy or BLEU, relying on static benchmarks and abstract task formulations no longer work as well in light of models' surprisingly robust superficial natural language understanding ability. We thus need to rethink how we design our benchmarks and evaluate our models so that they can still serve as useful indicators of progress going forward. This post aims to give an overview of challenges and opportunities in benchmarking in NLP, together with some general recommendations. I tried to cover perspectives from recent papers, talks at ACL 2021 as well as at the ACL 2021 Workshop on Benchmarking: Past, Present and Future , in addition to some of my own thoughts. Header image: Performance on SQuAD 2.0 over time (Credit: Papers with Code ) Table of contents: What is a benchmark? A brief history of benchmarking Metrics matter Consider the downstream use case Fine-grained evaluation The long tail of benchmark performance Large-scale continuous evaluation What is a benchmark? "Datasets are the telescopes of our field." — Aravind Joshi The original use of the term refers to horizontal marks made by surveyors in stone structures, into which an angle-iron could be placed to form a "bench" for a leveling rod . Figuratively, a benchmark refers to a standard point of reference against which things can be compared. A benchmark as it is used in ML or NLP typically has several components: it consists of one or multiple datasets, one or multiple associated metrics, and a way to aggregate performance. A benchmark sets a standard for assessing the performance of different systems that is agreed upon by the community. To ensure that a benchmark is accepted by the community, many recent benchmarks either select a representative set of standard tasks, such as GLUE or XTREME or actively solicit task proposals from the community, such as SuperGLUE , GEM , or BIG-Bench . For people in the field, benchmarks are crucial tools to track progress. Aravind Joshi said that without benchmarks to assess the performance of our models, we are just like "astronomers wanting to see the stars but refusing to build telescopes". For practitioners and outsiders, benchmarks provide an objective lens into a field that enables them to identify useful models and keep track of a field's progress. For instance, the AI Index Report 2021 uses SuperGLUE and SQuAD as a proxy for overall progress in natural language processing. Reaching human performance on influential benchmarks is often seen as a key milestone for a field. AlphaFold 2 reaching performance competitive with experimental methods on the CASP 14 competition marked a major scientific advance in the field of structural biology . A brief history of benchmarking "Creating good benchmarks is harder than most imagine." —John R. Mashey; foreword to Systems Benchmarking (2020) Benchmarks have a long history of being used to assess the performance of computational systems . The Standard Performance Evaluation Corporation ( SPEC ), established in 1988 is one of the oldest organisations dedicated to benchmarking the performance of computer hardware. Crucially, SPEC had support from most important companies in the field. Every year, it would release different benchmark sets, each composed of multiple programs, with performance measured as the geometric mean of millions of instructions per second ( MIPS ). A recent ML-specific analogue to SPEC is MLCommons , which organises the MLPerf series of performance benchmarks focusing on model training and inference. Similar to SPEC, MLPerf has a broad base of support from academia and industry, building on previous individual efforts for measuring performance such as DeepBench by Baidu or DAWNBench by Stanford. For US agencies such as DARPA and NIST , benchmarks played a crucial role in measuring and tracking scientific progress. Early benchmarks for automatic speech recognition (ASR) such as TIMIT and Switchboard were funded by DARPA and coordinated by NIST starting in 1986. Later influential benchmarks in other areas of ML such as MNIST were also based on NIST data. For language technology and information retrieval (IR), NIST ran the DARPA-funded TREC series of workshops covering a wide array of tracks and topics, which can be seen below. TREC organised competitions built on an evaluation paradigm pioneered by Cranfield in the 1960s where models are evaluated based on a set of test collections, consisting of documents, questions, and human relevance judgements. As the variance in performance across topics is large, scores are averaged over many topics. TREC's "standard, widely available, and carefully constructed set of data laid the groundwork for further innovation" ( Varian, 2008 ) in IR. Tasks and topics in the TREC workshops from 1992–2020 (Credit: Ellen Voorhees ) Many recent influential benchmarks such as ImageNet , SQuAD , or SNLI are large in scale, consisting of hundreds of thousands of examples and were developed by academic groups at well-funded universities. In the era of deep learning, such large-scale datasets have been credited as one of the pillars driving progress in research, with fields such as NLP or biology witnessing their 'ImageNet moment' . As models have become more powerful and general-purpose, benchmarks have become more application-oriented and increasingly moved from single-task to multi-task and

Sebastian Ruder examines how NLP models have saturated existing benchmarks so quickly that traditional single-metric evaluation approaches no longer effectively measure progress. He argues the field must rethink benchmark design through dynamic, adversarial, and multi-task approaches that better reflect real-world capabilities and resist rapid saturation.

directClaims: Models in NLP have become much more powerful driven by advances in transfer learning, Recent models have reached super-human performance on standard benchmarks such as SuperGLUE and SQuAD, Traditional practices for evaluating NLP performance using a single metric such as accuracy or BLEU, relying on static benchmarks and abstract task formulations no longer work as well, A benchmark consists of one or multiple datasets, one or multiple associated metrics, and a way to aggregate performance, Models have achieved superhuman performance on recent benchmarks such as GLUE and SQuAD 2.0 within about a year of their release, It took more than 15 years to achieve superhuman performance on classic benchmarks such as MNIST or Switchboard, In SNLI, annotators have been shown to rely on heuristics allowing models to make correct predictions using the hypothesis alone, Models trained on SQuAD are subject to adversarially inserted sentences

inferredBeliefs: The field needs to fundamentally rethink benchmark design and evaluation methodology, Static benchmarks are insufficient for tracking progress as model capabilities advance, Community consensus and broad participation are essential for benchmark legitimacy, Dynamic and adversarial benchmarks represent a necessary evolution in evaluation practices, Benchmarks serve as critical infrastructure for scientific progress in NLP

A sandboxed target, inputs that influence task difficulty, tools, and a grader.

Building effective cybersecurity evaluations requires a core pattern consisting of a sandboxed target environment, configurable inputs to vary task difficulty, available tools, and a grading mechanism. This architecture provides a systematic foundation for constructing reliable security assessments.

How to Work and Compound with AI

Context as infra, taste as config, verification for autonomy, scale via delegation, closing the loop.

AI work compounds through five interconnected principles: treating context as foundational infrastructure, encoding human taste into system configuration, implementing verification mechanisms for safe autonomy, scaling through delegation, and maintaining feedback loops. This framework positions AI effectiveness as a structural and iterative challenge rather than purely a capability one.

items.document:
TimeProVe: Propose, then Verify for Efficient Long Video Temporal Reasoning in Activities of Daily Living

Authors: Arkaprava Sinha, Dominick Reilly, Siddharth Krishnan et al.

Long Video Question Answering (LVQA) requires identifying sparse, query-relevant evidence within hours-long untrimmed videos. Existing approaches either process videos densely with large vision-language models (VLMs), incurring prohibitive computational cost, or rely on sparse caption-based reasoning, which often misses temporally localized and motion-centric evidence. We introduce TimeProVe, a cost-efficient hybrid framework for temporally grounded reasoning in long videos. TimeProVe first employs lightweight modules to generate action-grounded answer--evidence hypotheses and subsequently invokes an expensive VLM only for targeted verification. The core of our framework lies in the Action-based Candidate Evidence (ACE) module, which converts temporally localized actions into query-conditioned candidate answers and supporting evidence windows through lightweight LLM reasoning. We further introduce OpenTSUBench (OTB), an open-ended benchmark designed to evaluate temporally grounded reasoning in real-world Activities of Daily Living (ADL) scenarios. Experiments show that TimeProVe outperforms the strongest baseline on OTB by 7.3%, while reducing VLM calls by 75% and inference cost by 93%. Furthermore, without explicit temporal grounding training, TimeProVe achieves competitive performance on Charades-STA, and reaches state-of-the-art results when enhanced with grounding VLMs.

TimeProVe introduces a hybrid framework for efficient temporal reasoning in long videos by using lightweight modules to propose action-grounded hypotheses before selective VLM verification. The framework achieves 7.3% improvement over baselines while reducing inference cost by 93%, and is evaluated on a new open-ended benchmark (OpenTSUBench) for Activities of Daily Living scenarios.

directClaims: TimeProVe outperforms the strongest baseline on OTB by 7.3%, TimeProVe reduces VLM calls by 75% and inference cost by 93%, The Action-based Candidate Evidence (ACE) module converts temporally localized actions into query-conditioned candidate answers, TimeProVe achieves competitive performance on Charades-STA without explicit temporal grounding training, Long Video Question Answering requires identifying sparse, query-relevant evidence within hours-long untrimmed videos

inferredBeliefs: Hybrid approaches combining lightweight and expensive models can achieve better efficiency-accuracy tradeoffs than dense processing, Action localization is a useful intermediate representation for temporal reasoning in long videos, Targeted VLM invocation is preferable to exhaustive video processing for cost efficiency, Real-world ADL scenarios require different evaluation approaches than existing benchmarks

mentionedTools: TimeProVe, Action-based Candidate Evidence (ACE) module, OpenTSUBench (OTB), Charades-STA, vision-language models (VLMs), LLM

topics: video-understanding, temporal-reasoning, long-video-qa, efficiency-optimization, action-localization, activities-of-daily-living, vision-language-models, grounding, benchmark

items.document:
How Transparent is DiffusionGemma?

Authors: Joshua Engels, Callum McDougall, Bilal Chughtai et al.

LLM reasoning transparency is a critical affordance for understanding model decisions, mitigating misuse and misalignment, and debugging surprising model behaviors. However, DiffusionGemma performs a larger fraction of its computation in a continuous latent space; does this make its reasoning less transparent? We study this question by decomposing transparency into two components: variable transparency, whether we understand intermediate snapshots of a model's computational state; and algorithmic transparency, whether we can use these snapshots to reconstruct the process by which the model arrived at its outputs. Naively, DiffusionGemma has poor variable transparency: its opaque serial depth, the amount of serial computation that occurs in between interpretable model states, seems at first 28.6X higher than the corresponding autoregressive Gemma 4 model. However, we show that we can map the information flowing between denoising steps through an interpretable token bottleneck with no decrease in downstream performance. Treating these intermediate states as interpretable reduces the opaque serial depth to just 1.1X that of Gemma 4. Algorithmic transparency is harder for diffusion models than for autoregressive models because all token predictions in the canvas can change at every denoising step, giving the model the power to implement complicated distributed algorithms during the denoising process. To begin bridging this gap, we conduct a suite of interpretability case studies, uncovering initial evidence of novel diffusion-specific phenomena such as non-chronological reasoning, token and sequence smearing, and intermediate-context reasoning. Finally, we test monitorability, a key application of transparency that measures whether model outputs are useful for downstream tasks. We find that DiffusionGemma is similarly monitorable to Gemma 4.

This paper investigates the transparency of DiffusionGemma by decomposing it into variable and algorithmic transparency, finding that while the model's opaque serial depth initially appears significantly higher than autoregressive baselines, it can be substantially reduced through interpretable token bottleneck representations. The authors conduct case studies revealing novel diffusion-specific phenomena and demonstrate that DiffusionGemma maintains comparable monitorability to standard autoregressive models.

directClaims: DiffusionGemma performs a larger fraction of its computation in a continuous latent space, DiffusionGemma's opaque serial depth initially appears 28.6X higher than autoregressive Gemma 4, Information flowing between denoising steps can be mapped through an interpretable token bottleneck with no decrease in downstream performance, Mapping intermediate states as interpretable reduces opaque serial depth to 1.1X that of Gemma 4, All token predictions in the canvas can change at every denoising step in diffusion models, DiffusionGemma exhibits non-chronological reasoning, token and sequence smearing, and intermediate-context reasoning, DiffusionGemma is similarly monitorable to Gemma 4

inferredBeliefs: Transparency is decomposable into measurable components: variable transparency and algorithmic transparency, Interpretable intermediate representations can be extracted from opaque latent computations without performance loss, Monitorability of model outputs is a key practical application of transparency research, Novel architectural paradigms like diffusion require specialized interpretability techniques distinct from autoregressive models, Serial depth in latent spaces is a quantifiable metric for understanding model opacity

Thinking in Boxes: 3D Editing in Real Images Made Easy

Authors: Pradhaan S Bhat, Naveen Chandra R, Rishubh Parihar et al.

Text and 2D-conditioning interfaces provide weak, ambiguous control over spatial transformations in image editing -- particularly under large object motions and camera changes. Prior work has used 3D primitives such as boxes, but only as loose conditioning signals indicating approximate object location rather than specifying the transformation. We instead use 3D boxes as structured specifications: the user provides the input and output boxes of the edit, casting editing as a well-posed geometry problem. This ``thinking in boxes'' interface, where each box face is color-coded to convey 3D orientation, gives precise control over translation, rotation, scaling, and viewpoint changes in real images while preserving scene and object identity, and recovering previously unseen object regions. To ground transformations in scene appearance, we introduce a depth-aligned planar floor as a global reference frame, shaded with depth-aware cues. Conditioned on this structure, an image generator produces consistent results under large transformations. Trained in two stages -- on synthetic multi-object scenes and a small set of real-world videos from Objectron -- the system generalizes to complex, in-the-wild real images. Our method operates directly on real photographs and substantially outperforms recent state-of-the-art methods on large 3D edits.

This paper presents a 3D box-based interface for editing real images with precise control over object transformations, where input and output boxes specify geometry changes while a depth-aligned floor provides global reference structure. The method, trained on synthetic multi-object scenes and real Objectron videos, achieves state-of-the-art results on large 3D edits in complex in-the-wild images while preserving scene identity.

directClaims: 3D boxes provide precise control over translation, rotation, scaling, and viewpoint changes in real images, The method preserves scene and object identity while recovering previously unseen object regions, A depth-aligned planar floor serves as a global reference frame with depth-aware cues, The system generalizes to complex, in-the-wild real images after training on synthetic scenes and Objectron videos, The method substantially outperforms recent state-of-the-art methods on large 3D edits

inferredBeliefs: 3D geometric specification is superior to text and 2D-conditioning for spatial control in image editing, Structured 3D primitives can ground image generation in scene geometry more effectively than loose conditioning signals, Two-stage training on synthetic data followed by real-world video data enables effective generalization, Global reference frames and depth-aware visual cues improve consistency under large transformations

Incorporating physical source parameters into microlensing modeling

Authors: Mateusz J. Mróz, Radosław Poleski, Andrzej Udalski et al.

Modeling of complex microlensing events suffers from many difficult-to-disentangle degeneracies. This is especially the case for orbital motion of the source in a binary system, the so-called xallarap effect. To address the degeneracies inherent in xallarap modeling, we developed a novel approach that directly samples the physical parameters of the source stars (initial mass, evolutionary phase, metallicity, distance, and reddening) during MCMC fitting. In our approach the physical parameters of the source are estimated using MIST stellar evolution models. This parametrization imposes astrophysical constraints that help identify the physically most probable solutions. We test our method on the complex microlensing event OGLE-2017-BLG-0114, which exhibits signatures that can be traced to the complexity of the source system. We successfully constrained the microlensing models, achieving improvements in the Einstein ring radius estimates by up to an order of magnitude in the case of binary source models.

This paper presents a novel MCMC-based approach for microlensing event modeling that directly samples physical parameters of source stars using MIST stellar evolution models to break degeneracies, particularly the xallarap effect in binary systems. Applied to OGLE-2017-BLG-0114, the method achieved order-of-magnitude improvements in Einstein ring radius estimation for binary source configurations.

directClaims: Modeling of complex microlensing events suffers from many difficult-to-disentangle degeneracies, Orbital motion of the source in a binary system causes the xallarap effect, Physical parameters of source stars (initial mass, evolutionary phase, metallicity, distance, and reddening) are directly sampled during MCMC fitting, MIST stellar evolution models are used to estimate physical parameters of the source, Improvements in Einstein ring radius estimates by up to an order of magnitude were achieved for binary source models, Method was tested on OGLE-2017-BLG-0114 microlensing event

inferredBeliefs: Incorporating astrophysical constraints from stellar evolution models improves microlensing parameter estimation, Physical parameter space sampling is more effective than pure mathematical parametrization for resolving degeneracies, Source star properties are fundamentally constraining for microlensing event interpretation, Integrating domain knowledge from stellar evolution enhances model identifiability

Structuring and Tokenizing Distributed User Interest Context for Generative Recommendation

Authors: Ruizhong Qiu, Yinglong Xia, Dongqi Fu et al.

Generative recommendation is an emerging paradigm that has shown promise in industrial recommendation systems, aiming to predict users' next interactions from their historical behaviors. At the core of generative recommendation lies item tokenization, which bridges item semantics and recommendation models. However, existing methods often struggle to effectively organize and inject complex user-behavioral and item-semantic contexts into recommendation models simultaneously. On the one hand, existing graph-based integration methods, such as graph serialization and graph neural networks, either suffer from scalability issues or exploit only local graph information. On the other hand, existing semantic tokenization methods typically rely on heuristics and lack explicit supervision signals, which may lead to inaccurate or suboptimal semantic representations. To address these limitations in user interest context modeling, we propose G2Rec, a scalable framework that unifies holistic graph-based user co-engagement modeling with semantic tokenization for industrial-scale generative recommendation. Overall, G2Rec enables recommendation models to capture holistic and semantically grounded user interest prototypes without requiring ground-truth user interests, thereby providing more comprehensive and accurate modeling of user behavior contexts in industrial sequential recommendation. Online deployment across product surfaces and extensive experiments on public datasets demonstrate the superiority of G2Rec over existing methods.

G2Rec is a scalable framework that unifies graph-based user co-engagement modeling with semantic tokenization for generative recommendation systems. The approach enables recommendation models to capture holistic and semantically grounded user interest prototypes without ground-truth supervision, demonstrating superior performance over existing methods in industrial deployment.

directClaims: Generative recommendation aims to predict users' next interactions from their historical behaviors, Existing graph-based integration methods suffer from scalability issues or exploit only local graph information, Existing semantic tokenization methods typically rely on heuristics and lack explicit supervision signals, G2Rec enables recommendation models to capture holistic and semantically grounded user interest prototypes without requiring ground-truth user interests, Online deployment across product surfaces and extensive experiments demonstrate G2Rec's superiority over existing methods

inferredBeliefs: Combining user co-engagement graph modeling with semantic tokenization is necessary for effective user interest representation, Scalability and semantic accuracy are critical design challenges in industrial recommendation systems, User interest prototypes can be learned without explicit ground-truth supervision through holistic graph and semantic signals, Item tokenization is a fundamental bridge between item semantics and generative recommendation models

On the Renormalization Group Flow of Active Flocks

Authors: Kevin T. Grosvenor, Subodh P. Patil

In this paper, we study the statistical field-theoretic renormalization of active flocks via the MSRDJ action formulation for stochastic systems, focusing on the Toner-Tu theory of `Malthusian flocks', or polar-ordered, momentum non-conserving active fluids where relaxation times for density fluctuations are so short that they can be eliminated as a hydrodynamic variable. Working in the limit of isotropic diffusion in two spatial dimensions, we compute the renormalization of the couplings and their anomalous dimensions to all orders, facilitated by a non-linear realization of a generalized \textit{Galileon} symmetry and its associated Ward identities. We find a range of behavior depending on the parameters of the theory. If $κ$ is the diffusion coefficient and $Δ$ is the variance of the noise, we find a line of fixed points and a marginal vertex instability at $Δ/κ= 2π$. This instability separates Gaussian, and strongly interacting, symmetry-protected gapless phases, realizing non-equilibrium critical behavior beyond conventional Wilson--Fisher criticality. The existence of gapless excitations in both phases can be traced to the soft (Adler zero) theorems associated with the generalized Galileon symmetry, and implies the persistence of long range order when $Δ/κ$ is below the critical value. We revisit and contextualize various claims and counter-claims in the literature in light of our findings, and discuss extensions of our analysis to anisotropic diffusion, and towards flocks where density fluctuations are reintroduced.

This paper applies renormalization group analysis via MSRDJ formalism to Toner-Tu active flock theory, computing anomalous dimensions to all orders using generalized Galileon symmetries. The authors identify a marginal instability at Δ/κ = 2π separating Gaussian and strongly interacting gapless phases with non-equilibrium criticality beyond Wilson-Fisher universality.

directClaims: Compute renormalization of couplings and anomalous dimensions to all orders in Toner-Tu theory of active flocks, Find a line of fixed points and marginal vertex instability at Δ/κ = 2π, Instability separates Gaussian and strongly interacting, symmetry-protected gapless phases, Gapless excitations in both phases traced to soft (Adler zero) theorems of generalized Galileon symmetry, Long range order persists when Δ/κ is below the critical value, Non-equilibrium critical behavior realized beyond conventional Wilson-Fisher criticality

inferredBeliefs: Renormalization group methods provide essential framework for understanding active matter phase transitions, Generalized Galileon symmetries and their Ward identities are fundamental organizing principles in active fluids, Non-equilibrium systems can exhibit critical phenomena distinct from equilibrium universality classes, Density fluctuations can be consistently eliminated as hydrodynamic variables under certain timescale separation limits

mentionedTools: MSRDJ action formulation, Toner-Tu theory, Renormalization group analysis, Ward identities, Galileon symmetry, Adler zero theorems, Wilson-Fisher criticality

topics: active-matter, renormalization-group, field-theory, phase-transitions, critical-phenomena, symmetry-protected-phases, non-equilibrium-systems, flocks

Current World Models Lack a Persistent State Core

Authors: Jinpeng Lu, Dexu Zhu, Haoyuan Shi et al.

World models are increasingly regarded as a decisive step toward artificial general intelligence, yet modeling the physical world demands more than rendering convincing frames on demand: it requires an internal world state that keeps evolving over time, decoupled from observation, so that objects endure and events run to their conclusions whether or not a camera is watching, much as the moon holds to its orbit when no one is looking. This requirement is a blind spot of existing benchmarks, which reward surface properties such as fidelity, motion, and camera controllability while never asking whether a generated world keeps evolving once it is unobserved. We introduce \textbf{WRBench}, the first systematic diagnostic benchmark that treats camera motion as an intervention on observability and resolves evaluation into a human-calibrated chain that asks whether the camera executes the requested interaction, whether the scene stays continuous and identifiable while in view, and whether a returning target remains consistent with the event that was set in motion. Across 9{,}600 videos from 23 models spanning four control paradigms, one finding proves stubborn: current systems maintain the observed world as a tracking shot, resuming a returning target in the state at which it was abandoned rather than advancing the event while it went unseen. Because this failure recurs across control paradigms, model families, and increments of scale, robust world-state evolution does not follow from cleaner imagery, tighter control, richer geometric priors, or sheer parameter count We therefore argue that the stability of the physical state kernel and the consistency of worldlines under viewpoint intervention should become first-class objectives of world-model design, so that a world model captures how the world will unfold rather than how the next frame appears.

This paper identifies a fundamental limitation in current world models: they fail to maintain persistent physical state when objects leave view, instead treating the world as a tracking shot that pauses when unobserved. The authors introduce WRBench, the first diagnostic benchmark that exposes this failure across 23 models, demonstrating that robust world-state evolution cannot be achieved through improvements in image quality, control precision, or model scale alone.

directClaims: Current world models fail to maintain persistent state when objects are unobserved, resuming targets in their abandoned state rather than advancing events during occlusion, Existing benchmarks reward surface properties like fidelity, motion, and camera controllability while ignoring whether generated worlds continue evolving when unobserved, WRBench is the first systematic diagnostic benchmark treating camera motion as an intervention on observability, Evaluation across 9,600 videos from 23 models spanning four control paradigms shows robust world-state evolution does not follow from cleaner imagery, tighter control, richer geometric priors, or parameter count, The failure to maintain persistent world state recurs consistently across control paradigms, model families, and scales

inferredBeliefs: World models should maintain internal state decoupled from observation, mirroring how physical reality persists independently of viewpoint, Camera viewpoint is an intervention on observability rather than a passive observation mechanism, Stability of the physical state kernel and consistency of worldlines under viewpoint intervention should be first-class design objectives, Current optimization targets (frame fidelity, motion quality, control precision) are insufficient proxies for true world modeling capability

Controllable Quantum Spin Hall Phases in Bi$_2$Te$_3$-Family van der Waals Heterobilayers

Authors: Emmanuel V. C. Lopes, Pedro H. Sophia, Felipe Crasto de Lima et al.

The tunability and control of topological edge/surface states are crucial for the development of new device applications. In this work, by combining first-principles calculations and Wannier-based tight-binding methods, we show the emergence of quantum spin Hall phases in van der Waals heterostructures formed by stacking two trivial quintuple layers from the Bi$_2$Te$_3$ family. We demonstrate the tunability of the edge states under interlayer strain and external electric field effects, suggesting the possibility of switching topological edge states on/off by external control. Additionally, the quantum spin Hall edge channels remain robust against interlayer twist, highlighting their stability against external perturbations. Our results provide a new way to create and manipulate two-dimensional topological phases in systems based on Bi$_2$Te$_3$ family, which can be valuable for practical applications, such as topological field effect transistors and spintronic devices.

This work demonstrates the emergence of controllable quantum spin Hall phases in Bi2Te3-family van der Waals heterostructures through combined first-principles and tight-binding calculations. The study shows that topological edge states can be dynamically tuned via interlayer strain and electric fields while remaining robust to structural perturbations, enabling practical applications in topological devices and spintronics.

directClaims: Quantum spin Hall phases emerge in van der Waals heterostructures formed by stacking two trivial quintuple layers from the Bi2Te3 family, Edge states are tunable under interlayer strain and external electric field effects, Topological edge states can be switched on/off by external control, Quantum spin Hall edge channels remain robust against interlayer twist, Results provide a new way to create and manipulate two-dimensional topological phases in Bi2Te3-based systems

inferredBeliefs: Topological properties in van der Waals heterostructures can be engineered through external perturbations, Stacking trivial materials can give rise to non-trivial topological phases, Robustness against twist perturbations is essential for practical device implementation, Fine-tuning topological properties through multiple control parameters enables device applications

Caching for Dollars, Not Hits: An Exact Offline Reference for Cloud-Egress Caching and the Crossover That Decides When It Pays

Authors: Madhulatha Mandarapu, Sandeep Kunkunuru

When a cache miss fetches from cloud object storage, the bill is per GET request and per byte of egress, not latency. Classic caching minimizes the miss rate, the wrong objective: a rarely but expensively fetched object can cost thousands of times more dollars than a frequently but cheaply fetched one. Generalized-caching theory bounds the miss-cost objective, but no reported benchmark measures how far deployed heuristics sit from the dollar-optimal offline policy on real cloud prices. We supply that reference. For uniform-size page caches with heterogeneous miss costs the offline dollar-optimum is exact in polynomial time via an integral interval linear program -- validated against brute force; variable sizes are NP-hard, so we extend the flow-based offline bound from the hit-ratio objective to dollars (cost-FOO), tight to about four percent. Against this reference we find: (i) a heterogeneity-regret law -- LRU's dollar-regret rises with miss-cost dispersion (Spearman 0.87) while cost-aware GreedyDual cuts it to roughly a tenth; (ii) a contention frontier -- GreedyDual's residual regret collapses to near zero exactly when the budget fits the expensive working set, and is the open slice otherwise; and (iii) a closed-form crossover s* = GET_fee/egress_rate (about 4 KB on S3, 330 B on GCS) that predicts which deployments need dollar-aware caching at all. On a real Twitter trace the price vector alone moves the workload across s*, shifting the regime as predicted. The artifact is a reproducible billing-faithful benchmark; heuristics and bounds it builds on are prior work, credited.

This paper introduces an exact offline reference for cloud-egress caching that optimizes for dollar cost rather than hit rate, and establishes a closed-form crossover formula predicting when dollar-aware caching becomes necessary. The work reveals that cost-aware policies like GreedyDual significantly outperform hit-ratio optimized policies like LRU, with regret collapsing to near-zero at a specific budget-workset inflection point.

directClaims: Classic caching minimizes miss rate, which is the wrong objective for cloud storage billing, A rarely but expensively fetched object can cost thousands of times more dollars than a frequently but cheaply fetched one, For uniform-size page caches with heterogeneous miss costs, the offline dollar-optimum is exact in polynomial time via an integral interval linear program, Variable-size caches with heterogeneous miss costs are NP-hard, LRU's dollar-regret rises with miss-cost dispersion (Spearman 0.87) while cost-aware GreedyDual cuts it to roughly a tenth, GreedyDual's residual regret collapses to near zero exactly when the budget fits the expensive working set, A closed-form crossover s* = GET_fee/egress_rate (about 4 KB on S3, 330 B on GCS) predicts which deployments need dollar-aware caching, Cost-FOO bound is tight to about four percent for variable-size caches

inferredBeliefs: Cloud billing models fundamentally differ from traditional latency-based performance metrics and require different optimization objectives, Heterogeneous miss costs create a distinct problem space from uniform-cost caching theory, Cost-awareness should be a primary design consideration for cloud storage systems rather than an afterthought, Empirical validation against real cloud pricing is necessary to assess practical caching policies

mentionedTools: LRU, GreedyDual, S3, GCS, Twitter trace

topics: caching, cloud-storage, cost-optimization, offline-algorithms, billing, benchmark, egress-pricing

Execution-State Capsules: Graph-Bound Execution-State Checkpoint and Restore for Low-Latency, Small-Batch, On-Device Physical-AI Serving

Authors: Liang Su

Mainstream LLM serving systems reuse prefix work mainly through paged or radix key-value (KV) caches. This is highly effective for high-throughput, high-concurrency serving, but it manages only one positional fragment of execution state: the KV cache. We study the opposite regime: low-latency, small-batch, on-device physical-AI serving, where interactive LLM agents, speech systems, and robot policies repeatedly branch, reset, interrupt, and re-enter under tight responsiveness budgets. We introduce execution-state capsules, a graph-bound checkpoint and restore mechanism for the complete restorable state at a committed boundary. FlashRT is a white-box, backend-facing kernel runtime whose evaluated NVIDIA CUDA backend runs captured graph plans over contiguous static buffers with no block-table indirection. Because the live state is a closed set of named buffers, a capsule can snapshot, restore, fork, or roll back the whole execution boundary, including KV, recurrent state, convolution state, MTP state, and metadata. This moves reuse from token-addressed KV fragments to graph-bound execution-state boundaries. On an RTX 5090, capsule restore is byte-exact at the stored-state level and token-identical under greedy decode. A KV-only ablation diverges, showing that recurrent state is load-bearing. GPU-resident snapshot and restore are sub-millisecond, and TTFT speedup over cold prefill grows from 3.9x at 2k tokens to 27x at 16k tokens. On Jetson AGX Thor and DGX Spark, the same correctness and structural properties hold. Capsules are not a replacement for high-throughput KV-cache serving; they define a complementary latency-first serving point for explicit execution-state reuse.

Execution-state capsules introduce a graph-bound checkpoint-restore mechanism for low-latency, small-batch on-device LLM serving that manages complete execution state beyond just KV caches, enabling rapid branching and context switching for interactive agents and robot policies. FlashRT, the white-box kernel runtime implementing this approach, achieves sub-millisecond restore times and up to 27x speedup in time-to-first-token on GPUs while maintaining byte-exact correctness and demonstrating that recurrent state management is essential for proper system behavior.

directClaims: Execution-state capsules enable graph-bound checkpoint and restore of complete restorable state at committed boundaries, FlashRT runs captured graph plans over contiguous static buffers with no block-table indirection, Capsule restore is byte-exact at stored-state level and token-identical under greedy decode, GPU-resident snapshot and restore are sub-millisecond, TTFT speedup over cold prefill grows from 3.9x at 2k tokens to 27x at 16k tokens on RTX 5090, KV-only ablation diverges, showing recurrent state is load-bearing, Capsules handle KV cache, recurrent state, convolution state, MTP state, and metadata

inferredBeliefs: Low-latency, small-batch serving requires different optimization strategies than high-throughput serving, Complete execution state management is necessary for interactive systems with branching, reset, and interruption patterns, White-box kernel runtimes with static buffer management provide more control than token-addressed KV fragment approaches, Recurrent and stateful components beyond KV caches are critical for modern physical AI systems, Graph-bound execution semantics enable deterministic and byte-exact restoration guarantees

JanusMesh: Fast and Zero-Shot 3D Visual Illusion Generation via Cross-Space Denoising

Authors: Siang-Ling Zhang, Huai-Hsun Cheng, Tsung-Ju Yang et al.

Creating 3D visual illusions, a single 3D mesh that reveals entirely different semantics from various viewing angles, is a fascinating but tough challenge. Existing optimization-based methods are slow and can produce oversaturated colors. In contrast, naive stitching approaches fail to produce geometrically coherent objects. This results in visible unnatural seams and semantic leaks. In this paper, we present a fast and training-free framework for generating text-driven 3D visual illusions. Our approach decouples the generation into two stages. First, we propose a cross-space dual-branch denoising process. This process dynamically decodes 3D latents into voxel space for CLIP-guided orientation alignment and Signed Distance Field (SDF) blending, which ensures seamless geometric fusion. Second, we introduce a view-conditioned texture synthesis module that projects and aggregates view-specific 2D diffusion priors onto the fused geometry. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method generates highly realistic, dual-semantic 3D illusions in just 3-5 minutes. It significantly outperforms existing methods in geometric integrity, semantic recognizability, and efficiency. Project page: https://siang1105.github.io/JanusMesh.github.io/

JanusMesh presents a training-free framework for generating 3D visual illusions that display different semantics from different viewing angles through cross-space dual-branch denoising and view-conditioned texture synthesis. The method achieves high-quality, geometrically coherent illusions in 3-5 minutes, significantly outperforming existing optimization-based and naive stitching approaches.

directClaims: JanusMesh generates text-driven 3D visual illusions that reveal entirely different semantics from various viewing angles, The method produces results in 3-5 minutes, Existing optimization-based methods are slow and produce oversaturated colors, Naive stitching approaches fail to produce geometrically coherent objects with visible unnatural seams and semantic leaks, The approach uses cross-space dual-branch denoising that dynamically decodes 3D latents into voxel space for CLIP-guided orientation alignment and SDF blending, A view-conditioned texture synthesis module projects and aggregates view-specific 2D diffusion priors onto fused geometry, The method significantly outperforms existing methods in geometric integrity, semantic recognizability, and efficiency

inferredBeliefs: Training-free approaches are preferable for fast 3D generation tasks, Decoupling generation into geometry fusion and texture synthesis stages improves quality and coherence, CLIP guidance and diffusion priors are effective for semantic control in 3D generation, SDF-based blending is superior to naive stitching for geometric coherence, Voxel space processing enables better multi-view semantic alignment

MemoryWAM: Efficient World Action Modeling with Persistent Memory

Authors: Sizhe Yang, Juncheng Mu, Tianming Wei et al.

Robust robotic manipulation in the real world requires not only an understanding of the current observation, but also memory and dynamics modeling. World action models (WAMs) possess these capabilities by jointly modeling visual foresight and actions conditioned on both current and historical observations, making them a promising paradigm for robotic manipulation. However, existing WAMs face a fundamental trade-off: methods with efficient inference typically condition only on a bounded window of recent observations and therefore struggle in non-Markovian environments, whereas methods that preserve long histories incur time and space costs that grow substantially with sequence length. To address this challenge, we introduce MemoryWAM, a world action model with efficient persistent memory. MemoryWAM uses a hybrid memory design that combines recent frames, event-boundary anchor frames, and compact gist tokens that summarize long-range history. A tailored attention mechanism enables retrieval of both detailed short-term context and compressed long-term context, supporting memory-dependent decision-making with reduced inference latency and GPU memory usage. Across long-horizon, memory-dependent manipulation tasks in both simulation and the real world, MemoryWAM outperforms strong vision-language-action (VLA) and WAM baselines while maintaining favorable computational efficiency.

MemoryWAM introduces a hybrid persistent memory architecture for world action models that efficiently balances short-term detailed context and compressed long-term history through event-boundary anchors and gist tokens. The approach outperforms existing VLA and WAM baselines on long-horizon, memory-dependent manipulation tasks while reducing inference latency and GPU memory usage.

directClaims: MemoryWAM uses a hybrid memory design combining recent frames, event-boundary anchor frames, and compact gist tokens that summarize long-range history, MemoryWAM outperforms strong vision-language-action (VLA) and WAM baselines while maintaining favorable computational efficiency, Existing WAMs face a fundamental trade-off between efficient inference with bounded observation windows and methods that preserve long histories with substantial time and space costs, A tailored attention mechanism enables retrieval of both detailed short-term context and compressed long-term context

inferredBeliefs: Robotic manipulation benefits from joint modeling of visual foresight and action dynamics, Non-Markovian environments require access to historical context beyond recent observations, Efficiency in robotic systems requires balancing memory capacity with computational constraints, Selective memory compression through event boundaries and gist tokens can preserve task-relevant information

UNIEGO: Proxies as Mediators for Unified Egocentric Video Representation Learning

Authors: Wenhao Chi, Arkaprava Sinha, Dominick Reilly et al.

Egocentric video understanding is inherently limited by the narrow perspective of wearable cameras: a single viewpoint, a single modality, a single model cannot capture the full richness of human action. We argue that a truly expressive egocentric representation must subsume complementary knowledge across viewpoints, modalities, and foundation model representations, yet remain deployable from egocentric video alone. To this end, we introduce a hierarchical multi-teacher distillation framework that produces UNIEGO, a unified egocentric encoder trained with nine teachers spanning ego-exo viewpoints, RGB, depth, and skeleton modalities, and four foundation models. Rather than distilling directly from heterogeneous teachers whose incompatible architectures and feature geometries induce conflicting gradients, our framework interposes a layer of representation-specific Proxy models that translate diverse teacher knowledge into a homogeneous egocentric space. A second distillation stage, Selective Proxy Distillation (SPD), then adaptively selects, for each training sample, the subset of proxies that are both correct and confident, distilling exclusively from reliable supervision and suppressing erroneous signals. SPD is further stabilized by initializing UNIEGO as a learned convex combination of proxy parameters, placing the unified model in a well-conditioned region of the loss landscape before distillation begins. UNIEGO achieves state-of-the-art performance across three egocentric video understanding tasks - action recognition, video retrieval, and action segmentation on three challenging ego-exo benchmarks, outperforming naive multi-teacher distillation baselines and demonstrating that structured, proxy-mediated knowledge transfer yields richer and more discriminative egocentric representations.

UNIEGO is a unified egocentric video encoder trained via hierarchical multi-teacher distillation across nine teachers spanning different viewpoints, modalities, and foundation models. The key innovation is using representation-specific proxy models as mediators to translate heterogeneous teacher knowledge into a homogeneous egocentric space, with selective proxy distillation adaptively choosing reliable supervision sources per sample, achieving state-of-the-art results on action recognition, video retrieval, and action segmentation tasks.

directClaims: A truly expressive egocentric representation must subsume complementary knowledge across viewpoints, modalities, and foundation model representations, yet remain deployable from egocentric video alone, UNIEGO is trained with nine teachers spanning ego-exo viewpoints, RGB, depth, and skeleton modalities, and four foundation models, Proxy models translate diverse teacher knowledge into a homogeneous egocentric space to avoid conflicting gradients from heterogeneous teachers, Selective Proxy Distillation (SPD) adaptively selects, for each training sample, the subset of proxies that are both correct and confident, UNIEGO achieves state-of-the-art performance across three egocentric video understanding tasks: action recognition, video retrieval, and action segmentation, Initializing UNIEGO as a learned convex combination of proxy parameters places the unified model in a well-conditioned region of the loss landscape

inferredBeliefs: Multi-modal and multi-viewpoint knowledge is necessary to overcome the inherent limitations of single-viewpoint egocentric video, Direct distillation from multiple heterogeneous teachers with incompatible architectures leads to conflicting optimization signals, Intermediate representation spaces (proxies) can effectively harmonize knowledge from diverse sources, Sample-level adaptive selection of supervision sources improves robustness and prevents error propagation, Careful initialization in parameter space can improve the optimization landscape for distillation

Optimal Deterministic Multicalibration and Omniprediction

Authors: Georgy Noarov, Aaron Roth

A model is multicalibrated on a collection of group weights $G$ if it is calibrated -- i.e. unbiased even conditional on its prediction -- not just overall, but also after reweighting contexts by each $g \in G$. It is a useful property for many downstream applications and is a basic desideratum of trustworthy machine learning. Before this work, all predictors known to attain the minimax-optimal $\widetilde O(\varepsilon^{-3})$ sample complexity rate for $\varepsilon$-multicalibration were randomized, while deterministic predictors were known only with substantially worse sample complexity. Whether randomization is necessary for optimal sample complexity in multicalibration was explicitly asked by [CLNR26] and implicitly in several prior works. We resolve this open problem by giving a minimax-optimal multicalibration algorithm that outputs a deterministic predictor. We then generalize the algorithm to produce optimal deterministic predictors that satisfy outcome indistinguishability (OI) with respect to finite or finitely covered collections of tests. As an application, this also gives deterministic omnipredictors and panpredictors with optimal sample complexity, resolving open problems posed by [OKK25] and [BHHLZ25].

This work resolves an open problem by constructing the first deterministic multicalibration algorithm achieving minimax-optimal Õ(ε^-3) sample complexity, proving randomization is unnecessary. The approach generalizes to produce optimal deterministic predictors satisfying outcome indistinguishability, omniprediction, and panprediction properties.

directClaims: All predictors known to attain minimax-optimal Õ(ε^-3) sample complexity for ε-multicalibration were randomized before this work, Deterministic predictors were known only with substantially worse sample complexity than randomized ones, This work gives a minimax-optimal multicalibration algorithm that outputs a deterministic predictor, The algorithm generalizes to produce optimal deterministic predictors satisfying outcome indistinguishability with respect to finite or finitely covered collections of tests, This resolves open problems for deterministic omnipredictors and panpredictors with optimal sample complexity

inferredBeliefs: Randomization is not inherently necessary for achieving optimal sample complexity in multicalibration, Deterministic predictors are preferable to randomized ones for trustworthy machine learning applications, Sample complexity lower bounds and algorithmic upper bounds should match, Multicalibration and outcome indistinguishability are related properties with unified algorithmic approaches

mentionedTools: CLNR26, OKK25, BHHLZ25

From Efficiency to Leakage -- Privacy Backdoor in Federated Language Model Fine-Tuning

Authors: Shanghao Shi, Chaoyu Zhang, Heng Jin et al.

Federated learning (FL) enables multiple parties to collaboratively fine-tune language models for domain-specific tasks without sharing raw data. Since full model fine-tuning is often prohibitively expensive for FL clients, parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) has become the de facto approach in practice, freezing the base model and training only a small set of adapters. In this paper, we show that a malicious parameter server can stealthily corrupt a PEFT adapter into a privacy backdoor that implicitly memorizes the client's training samples as isolated per-sample parameter updates stored in separate neurons, without degrading model utility. Concretely, our attack, NeuroImprint, assigns a dedicated memorization neuron to each training sample and constrains that each neuron is updated at most once along the local fine-tuning trajectory. This design mitigates both cross-sample collisions and cross-step mixing introduced by large local batches and stateful optimizers (e.g., Adam/AdamW) in language-model fine-tuning. After fine-tuning, the resulting isolated per-sample updates can be analytically inverted in closed form to recover text embeddings, which are then deterministically mapped back to token sequences. To understand the generality of our method, we implemented NeuroImprint on multiple language models (BERT, GPT-2, Qwen2, and Llama3.2) and evaluated it across four fine-tuning datasets spanning diverse domains. The results demonstrate that our attack can reconstruct 59% to 79% of all finetuning samples with high semantic fidelity.

This paper demonstrates a privacy backdoor attack called NeuroImprint that enables a malicious parameter server to implicitly memorize client training samples during federated parameter-efficient fine-tuning of language models without degrading utility. The attack achieves 59-79% reconstruction of fine-tuning samples by assigning dedicated neurons to each sample and analytically inverting parameter updates to recover text embeddings.

directClaims: A malicious parameter server can stealthily corrupt a PEFT adapter into a privacy backdoor that implicitly memorizes client training samples as isolated per-sample parameter updates stored in separate neurons without degrading model utility, NeuroImprint assigns a dedicated memorization neuron to each training sample and constrains that each neuron is updated at most once along the local fine-tuning trajectory, The attack can reconstruct 59% to 79% of all fine-tuning samples with high semantic fidelity, Isolated per-sample updates can be analytically inverted in closed form to recover text embeddings, which are then deterministically mapped back to token sequences, The design mitigates both cross-sample collisions and cross-step mixing introduced by large local batches and stateful optimizers in language-model fine-tuning

inferredBeliefs: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning in federated learning creates a vulnerability that current defenses do not adequately address, Per-sample parameter isolation is an effective mechanism for privacy-preserving attack design, Closed-form inversion of neural network updates is feasible for extracting meaningful training data, Malicious parameter servers represent a credible threat model in federated language model fine-tuning

mentionedTools: BERT, GPT-2, Qwen2, Llama3.2, Adam, AdamW, NeuroImprint

topics: federated-learning, privacy-attacks, backdoor-attacks, parameter-efficient-fine-tuning, membership-inference, data-extraction, language-models, security

Generating Robot Hands from Human Demonstrations

Authors: Sha Yi, Nicklas Hansen, Xueqian Bai et al.

Robot learning has advanced rapidly in learning control, but learning the physical body of a robot remains much more difficult because jointly searching over design and control creates a very large combinatorial problem. Here, we present a data-driven framework for generating robot hands from human demonstrations. Instead of learning a complex controller together with each candidate design, we generate robot hand designs using the same simple control policy used after fabrication: matching fingertip positions through inverse kinematics. Using more than 4 million frames of human fingertip motion from everyday manipulation, our algorithm optimizes tree-structured robot hands to reproduce desired target motions. The framework produced both a 6-degree-of-freedom (DoF) general-purpose hand and lower-DoF task-specific hands with spatial four-bar mimic joints. To accelerate the search over designs, we trained a reinforcement-learning (RL) actor to propose good hand designs and joint angles, reducing search time from hours to minutes. We fabricated the mechanisms directly as one-piece articulated structures with print-in-place joints. In real-world experiments, the 6-DoF hand achieved highly accurate teleoperated fingertip tracking better than available commercial robot hands, whereas the specialized 3-DoF hands reproduced structured human and synthetic trajectories with reduced mechanical complexity. These results showed that large-scale human motion data can be used not only to train robot controllers but also as a reference for optimizing and generating the physical embodiment of robots.

This paper presents a data-driven framework that generates optimized robot hand designs by leveraging 4+ million frames of human manipulation demonstrations and inverse kinematics control, rather than jointly learning both morphology and control. The resulting 6-DoF hand and task-specific variants demonstrated superior teleoperated fingertip tracking and motion reproduction compared to commercial alternatives, showing that large-scale human motion data can effectively guide robot embodiment design.

directClaims: A data-driven framework generates robot hand designs from human demonstrations using inverse kinematics as a simple control policy, The algorithm was trained on more than 4 million frames of human fingertip motion from everyday manipulation, A 6-DoF general-purpose hand and lower-DoF task-specific hands with spatial four-bar mimic joints were produced, RL-based actor reduced design search time from hours to minutes, The 6-DoF hand achieved highly accurate teleoperated fingertip tracking better than available commercial robot hands, Fabricated mechanisms were created as one-piece articulated structures with print-in-place joints

inferredBeliefs: Decoupling design optimization from control policy learning reduces combinatorial complexity in robot embodiment design, Human motion data provides sufficient information to optimize both kinematic structure and task performance, Task-specific designs with reduced DoF can outperform general-purpose designs on specialized manipulation tasks, Inverse kinematics-based control is sufficient for complex manipulation when paired with appropriately designed morphology

mentionedTools: Inverse kinematics, Reinforcement learning actor, Tree-structured hand optimization

Benchmark of quantum algorithms for ground state preparation in the presence of noise

Authors: Daniel Molpeceres, Sirui Lu, J. Ignacio Cirac et al.

We compare the performance of representative cooling, adiabatic, and optimization algorithms for ground-state preparation in the presence of noise. Using an exactly solvable family of quadratic fermionic Hamiltonians subject to depolarizing noise, we derive the scaling of the achievable relative energy as a function of the noise rate and support these results with numerical simulations. The Hamiltonian exhibits two phases, separated by a quantum phase transition. As expected, the performance of the different algorithms depends on the phase: adiabatic evolution is favorable in the trivial phase, while a multi-frequency cooling algorithm, as proposed in [1], becomes competitive or superior in the topological phase, where gap-closing limits adiabatic protocols. We further present numerical results for the quantum approximate optimization algorithm [2], showing that it performs competitively with cooling in the trivial phase but is typically outperformed in the topological regime. Finally, we show that for this model the cooling protocol exhibits enhanced robustness to parameter imperfections, highlighting its potential advantage for realistic implementations of noisy quantum state preparation. The analytical approach developed here, in conjunction with numerical validation, establishes an extendable approach to benchmarking ground-state preparation algorithms.

This paper benchmarks representative cooling, adiabatic, and optimization algorithms for noisy ground-state preparation using exactly solvable quadratic fermionic Hamiltonians subject to depolarizing noise. The study reveals phase-dependent algorithm performance across a quantum phase transition, with cooling protocols showing superior robustness to parameter imperfections in the topological regime and establishing an extensible framework for benchmarking quantum state preparation algorithms.

directClaims: Adiabatic evolution is favorable in the trivial phase for ground-state preparation under noise, Multi-frequency cooling algorithms become competitive or superior in the topological phase where gap-closing limits adiabatic protocols, Quantum approximate optimization algorithm performs competitively with cooling in the trivial phase but is typically outperformed in the topological regime, Cooling protocol exhibits enhanced robustness to parameter imperfections compared to other algorithms, Performance of ground-state preparation algorithms depends on the phase of the Hamiltonian separated by quantum phase transition, Achievable relative energy scales as a function of noise rate in quadratic fermionic Hamiltonians under depolarizing noise

inferredBeliefs: Different quantum algorithms have phase-dependent suitability for noisy ground-state preparation, Robustness to parameter imperfections is a critical criterion for practical quantum algorithm implementation, Exactly solvable models provide valuable insights for benchmarking quantum algorithms in realistic noise conditions, Systematic comparison of multiple algorithm classes is necessary to understand their relative merits under noise

mentionedTools: Quantum approximate optimization algorithm (QAOA), Multi-frequency cooling algorithm, Adiabatic evolution protocols, Quadratic fermionic Hamiltonians

Easy Reads: A Python program for making Scientific Papers on arXiv more Reader Friendly and Accessible

Authors: Vishal Verma

Scientific papers are frequently dense and characterized by features such as small fonts and line spacing, double columns of text, and tightly arranged figures. While these features make papers more compact, they can hinder readability, make them less accessible, and can strain the reader. arXiv is a premier open-access repository for scientific papers across different fields and is used extensively by researchers, including those in the physics and astrophysics communities. Easy Reads is an automated, end-to-end, open-source Python program that helps address the stated challenge by making papers from arXiv more reader-friendly and accessible. Easy Reads can automatically fetch a paper from arXiv via its URL and work with the source TeX file to allow custom formatting of the paper features, primarily the font size, and the number of columns used. The main goal of Easy Reads is to facilitate ease of reading of scientific papers.

Easy Reads is an open-source Python tool that automatically retrieves scientific papers from arXiv and reformats their TeX source to improve readability through customizable font sizes and column layouts. The tool addresses the accessibility challenges posed by dense, compact paper formatting that strains readers.

directClaims: Scientific papers are frequently dense with small fonts, line spacing, double columns of text, and tightly arranged figures that hinder readability and accessibility, Easy Reads is an automated, end-to-end, open-source Python program that makes arXiv papers more reader-friendly and accessible, Easy Reads can automatically fetch papers from arXiv via URL and work with source TeX files to allow custom formatting, Easy Reads enables customization of font size and number of columns in scientific papers

Topological Codes Based on Space Groups

Authors: Chong-Yuan Xu, Ze-Chuan Liu, Yong Xu

Topological codes form one of the most important classes of stabilizer codes. Most existing algebraic constructions and analyses of topological codes assume translation invariance. Here we show that topological codes can arise in more general settings by incorporating point group operations. The central construction is a class of Calderbank-Shor-Steane (CSS) codes called space-group codes, whose check operators are built from group-algebra templates over space groups that combine translations with point-group operations. We develop methods for analyzing topological properties of space-group codes using ring-modules and their invariant theory. At first glance, space-group codes might appear to complicate practical implementation; however, we find that they can exhibit greater locality than previous codes based purely on translations. Our framework thus extends the landscape of topological codes and opens up a broader design space for the co-design of topological codes with quantum computing platforms.

This paper introduces space-group codes, a generalization of topological codes that incorporate point group operations alongside translations by leveraging group-algebra templates over space groups. The authors demonstrate that this framework enables topological codes with greater locality than translation-only constructions, expanding the design space for practical quantum error correction.

directClaims: Topological codes can arise in more general settings by incorporating point group operations, Space-group codes are a class of CSS codes whose check operators are built from group-algebra templates over space groups, Space-group codes can exhibit greater locality than previous codes based purely on translations, The framework extends the landscape of topological codes and opens up a broader design space for co-design with quantum computing platforms

inferredBeliefs: Translation invariance is an unnecessarily restrictive assumption for topological code construction, Point group symmetries can be leveraged to improve practical properties of stabilizer codes, Locality properties are a key consideration for implementation feasibility of topological codes, Ring-module theory and invariant theory provide useful tools for analyzing topological codes

mentionedTools: Calderbank-Shor-Steane (CSS) codes, stabilizer codes, ring-modules, invariant theory, group-algebra templates

CalTennis: Large Multi-View Tennis Video Dataset and Benchmark of Monocular-to-3D Pose Estimation

Authors: Ilona Demler, Xinran Xie, Blake Werner et al.

The Caltech Tennis Dataset (CalTennis) is a large-scale video benchmark for evaluating monocular-to-3D pose estimation in the wild. CalTennis comprises over 11 million frames (51 hours) of tennis practice and match play from 40 players, captured with 2-6 synchronized cameras at 60 Hz. It is 10 times larger than existing in-the-wild human motion video datasets and 3 times larger than existing MOCAP-ground-truthed datasets, and it is the first large-scale benchmark to provide synchronized multi-view recordings of expert athletic motion. The multi-view setup enables inexpensive, label-free evaluation of monocular-to-3D pose estimation algorithms. We describe a simple, standardized protocol that enables data collection without specialized equipment or expertise, along with fully automated video calibration and synchronization. Benchmarking state-of-the-art monocular-to-3D pose methods on CalTennis, we find that while 3D joint angle recovery is now quite accurate, all models struggle to estimate depth and foot contact consistently. We further propose two novel performance metrics, footwork and stability, as well as qualitatively study body shape inconsistency. These metrics expose previously underexplored failure modes and point to concrete opportunities for improvement in pose estimation and action analysis.

CalTennis introduces a large-scale multi-view tennis video dataset (11M+ frames from 40 players) for evaluating monocular 3D pose estimation in expert athletic motion, with synchronized cameras enabling label-free ground truth evaluation. Benchmarking state-of-the-art methods reveals that while 3D joint angles are accurate, depth and foot contact estimation remain challenging, and the paper proposes novel footwork and stability metrics to expose these failure modes.

directClaims: CalTennis comprises over 11 million frames (51 hours) of tennis practice and match play from 40 players, Captured with 2-6 synchronized cameras at 60 Hz, 10 times larger than existing in-the-wild human motion video datasets, 3 times larger than existing MOCAP-ground-truthed datasets, First large-scale benchmark to provide synchronized multi-view recordings of expert athletic motion, Multi-view setup enables inexpensive, label-free evaluation of monocular-to-3D pose estimation algorithms, 3D joint angle recovery is now quite accurate, but all models struggle to estimate depth and foot contact consistently, Proposed two novel performance metrics: footwork and stability

Observation of electroweak production of pairs of Z bosons in proton-proton collisions at 13 TeV

Authors: CMS Collaboration

The first evidence of electroweak (EW) production of pairs of Z bosons in association with two jets (jj) in the final state ZZjj $\to$ $\ell\ellνν$jj, where $\ell$ = e, $μ$, is reported by the CMS experiment. The analysis is based on a data sample of proton-proton (pp) collisions at $\sqrt{s}$ = 13 TeV, corresponding to an integrated luminosity of 138 fb$^{-1}$. Events are selected by requiring exactly two leptons of same flavor and opposite charge, large missing transverse momentum, and two jets with a large rapidity separation and large invariant mass. The EW production cross section in a fiducial volume is $σ_{\mathrm{EW}}$(pp $\to$ ZZjj $\to$ $\ell\ellνν$jj) = 0.37$^{+0.14}_{-0.12}$ (stat)$^{+0.06}_{-0.06}$ (syst) fb, in agreement with the standard model prediction of 0.39 $\pm$ 0.06 fb. The observed (expected) significance of the signal is 3.1 (2.8) standard deviations. Limits on anomalous quartic gauge couplings are derived in terms of dimension-8 effective field theory operators. A combination with the previously reported result from the ZZ decay channel with four charged leptons yields an observed (expected) significance of 5.0 (4.5) standard deviations for the EW production of Z boson pairs.

The CMS collaboration reports the first evidence of electroweak Z boson pair production with two jets at 13 TeV, measuring a cross section of 0.37 fb in agreement with standard model predictions with 3.1σ significance. Combined with the four-lepton decay channel, the combined significance reaches 5.0σ, and the analysis constrains anomalous quartic gauge couplings using effective field theory operators.

directClaims: First evidence of electroweak production of pairs of Z bosons in association with two jets (ZZjj) in the final state ZZjj → ℓℓνν jj, Analysis based on proton-proton collisions at √s = 13 TeV with integrated luminosity of 138 fb⁻¹, EW production cross section σ_EW(pp → ZZjj → ℓℓνν jj) = 0.37⁺⁰·¹⁴₋₀.₁₂ (stat)⁺⁰·⁰⁶₋₀.₀₆ (syst) fb, in agreement with SM prediction of 0.39 ± 0.06 fb, Observed significance of signal is 3.1 standard deviations (expected 2.8 σ), Combined with four-lepton ZZ channel yields observed significance of 5.0 σ (expected 4.5 σ) for EW Z boson pair production, Limits on anomalous quartic gauge couplings derived using dimension-8 effective field theory operators

inferredBeliefs: Vector boson scattering processes provide sensitive probes of electroweak symmetry breaking and gauge boson interactions, Precision measurements of rare processes require sophisticated event selection with kinematic constraints (rapidity separation, invariant mass), Effective field theory framework is appropriate for parameterizing beyond-standard-model physics in gauge coupling sector, Combination of complementary decay channels improves statistical significance for rare process observation

Toward Calibrated Mixture-of-Experts Under Distribution Shift

Authors: Gina Wong, Drew Prinster, Suchi Saria et al.

Calibration aligns a model's predictive uncertainty with the frequencies of its empirical outcomes and is important for understanding and trusting reported probabilities. Recent work shows that enforcing calibration at the level of individual predictors can improve ensemble accuracy and calibration, with mixture-of-experts (MoE) models showing strong empirical improvements in particular; however, the conditions under which calibration helps MoE are not well understood. In this work, we study how MoE models behave under distribution shift, focusing on how routing mechanisms interact with expert-level calibration. We show that expert calibration is sufficient to ensure calibration of the overall model under a broad class of distribution shifts in hard-routed models, but is insufficient for calibrating soft-routed models. To address this, we propose an adversarial reweighting that penalizes calibration errors of the routed aggregate under distribution shift, and we demonstrate that it improves the accuracy-calibration tradeoff both on average and on difficult subsets of the data, across model classes, prediction tasks, and distribution shifts.

This paper investigates how calibration interacts with routing mechanisms in mixture-of-experts models under distribution shift, revealing that expert-level calibration guarantees overall calibration for hard-routed but not soft-routed models. The authors propose an adversarial reweighting approach that improves the accuracy-calibration tradeoff across different MoE architectures and distribution shifts.

directClaims: Expert calibration is sufficient to ensure calibration of the overall model under a broad class of distribution shifts in hard-routed models, Expert calibration is insufficient for calibrating soft-routed models, Adversarial reweighting penalizing calibration errors of the routed aggregate under distribution shift improves the accuracy-calibration tradeoff, Enforcing calibration at the level of individual predictors can improve ensemble accuracy and calibration

inferredBeliefs: Routing mechanisms play a critical role in how calibration properties propagate through MoE architectures, Hard-routed and soft-routed experts have fundamentally different calibration requirements, Distribution shift poses distinct challenges for MoE calibration that differ from standard settings, Adversarial training approaches can address calibration failures in complex ensemble systems
